      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},

        },

        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "{\"cmd\":[\"bash\",\"-lc\",\"apply_patch <<'EOF'\\n*** Begin Patch\\n*** Update File: final_hybrid.txt\\n@@\\n The lower markets of Te’Oga, they ain't just a place; they breathe. Like\\n@@\\n- A beautiful, glorious, goddamn mess. Streets here don't give a damn\\n- about blueprints or straight lines. They twist and turn, just like folks\\n- who built 'em, worried more about a roof over their head than some fancy\\n- architect's perfect angles. Every cobblestone's got a story soaked into\\n- it—deals cut, hearts busted, and enough cheap liquor drank to float a\\n- battleship. Up high, you see those spires, all gleaming white, where the\\n- Order lives. They're built on the idea that if you climb high enough,\\n- you can leave all the world's bullshit behind. That's a lie, and every\\n- damn soul down here knows it. The air in these markets? Smells like\\n- coriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all\\n- crammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell\\n- of life, the real kind, un-sanitized, un-approved. I move through this\\n- crowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in\\n- my pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a\\n- world tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a\\n- machine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can\\n- be fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee-high to a\\n- grasshopper, even after the Foundry. \\\"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\\\"\\n+ A beautiful, glorious, goddamn mess. Streets here don't give a damn\\n+ about blueprints or straight lines. They twist and turn, just like folks\\n+ who built 'em, worried more about a roof over their head than some fancy\\n+ architect's perfect angles.\\n+\\n+      Sound moves strange in Te'Oga. The Order's spires above are coated in materials that drink up noise. There's no echo in these alleys. Laughter dies fast, jokes get swallowed before they can ripple down a block. Drop a wrench and it doesn't clang; it makes a dull, sterile thud that gets soaked up by the walls. Even joy feels like a thing the city is tryin' to suppress. It's unnerving as hell, and it's meant to be. The silence is engineered.\\n+\\n+ Every cobblestone's got a story soaked into\\n+ it—deals cut, hearts busted, and enough cheap liquor drank to float a\\n+ battleship. Up high, you see those spires, all gleaming white, where the\\n+ Order lives. They're built on the idea that if you climb high enough,\\n+ you can leave all the world's bullshit behind. That's a lie, and every\\n+ damn soul down here knows it.\\n+\\n+      The city even corrects you while you walk. Hidden speakers whisper through the bones of the street, not just calling for \\\"Harmony Assessments,\\\" but gently adjusting the citizens themselves. \\\"Citizen 7‑B, adjust spinal alignment by three degrees for optimal efficiency,\\\" a soft, automated voice will say as you pass. It'll remind you to tighten your stride, to reduce variance in your gait, to smile with the approved symmetry. It's like havin' a bureaucrat perched on your shoulder, sanding down every rough edge in real time.\\n+\\n+ The air in these markets? Smells like\\n+ coriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all\\n+ crammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell\\n+ of life, the real kind, un‑sanitized, un‑approved. I move through this\\n+ crowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in\\n+ my pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a\\n+ world tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a\\n+ machine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can\\n+ be fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee‑high to a\\n+ grasshopper, even after the Foundry. \\\"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\\\"\\n*** End Patch\\nEOF\\n\"],\"timeout\": 100000}"
        },

    "052bfa54-77e8-45fe-a50c-abe595c67109": {
      "children": [
        "3256867a-a6f0-485e-b8fc-7a08950dd28e"
      ],
      "id": "052bfa54-77e8-45fe-a50c-abe595c67109",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "grief-powered heart of the Folly. The ship was more than a machine now;\nit was a patient, and it was dyin'. Its sorrowful hum was gettin'\nweaker, its paradoxical heart strugglin' to beat in a world that was\nbein' systematically silenced or absorbed. But the real heart of our\nnew, quiet world was in the ship's small med-bay. I sat on the floor, my\nback against the cold metal wall, watchin' Ember. A ghost of myself, the\nsupernova of my grief havin' burned away the last of my innocence,\nleavin' behind a quiet, hollowed-out resolve. The cobalt fire still\nflickered in my eyes, but it was a low, steady flame now, a pilot light\nin the darkness of my soul. A pilot light for something much bigger.\nEmber was... changin'. The fall of Concordance, the psychic scream of my\nawakenin', had been a kick in the ass. The perfect, silent void that was\nthe kid was startin' to... expand. She sat on a medical cot, her small\nhands restin' in her lap, her dark eyes fixed on a point in the empty\nair. She wasn't just lookin' at the world anymore. She was connectin'\nwith it. \"She's listenin',\" I whispered, my voice a raw, quiet sound.\nDidn't know who I was talkin' to. Maybe myself. Maybe the ghosts. I\ncould feel it. A subtle, constant hum comin' off her, a frequency that\nwasn't a sound, but a question. It was the hum of a system reachin' out,\ntryin' to make contact. I remembered the Operator's logs Tiffani had\nshown me. A null-state variable. A key to the Debug Vault. On a\ndesperate, insane impulse, I reached out with my own will. Not with a\nblast of power, not with a command. I echoed her question. I took the\nquiet, steady hum of my own transformed soul and I sang it back to her,\na single, clear note of pure, focused intent. I'm here, the note said.\nI'm listenin'. For the first time, Ember's gaze shifted. Her dark, empty\neyes focused on me. And she answered. It wasn't a voice. It wasn't a\nthought. It was a data-stream. A torrent of pure, unmediated information\nflowed from her, into me, a river of light and logic and beautiful,\nterrible truth. I saw the universe as she saw it: not as a place, but as\na program. A vast, intricate piece of code, written in a language of\npure mathematics and divine will. I saw the core functions, the \"gods,\"\nas fightin' subroutines, their endless argument a recursive error that\nwas makin' the system fall apart. I saw the Operator, the ghost in the\nmachine, and I felt the cold, dispassionate logic of his final solution:\nthe Great Silence. A hard reset. A system wipe. And I saw myself.\nAnomaly 734. A bug in the system. A flaw. But I also saw what Tiffani\nhad seen, what Ember was showin' me now. I wasn't just a bug. I was a\npatch. A piece of user-created content that had the potential to rewrite\nthe source code. The communion lasted for a second or an eternity. When\nit was over, I was on the floor, gaspin', my mind reelin', a thin\ntrickle of blood from my nose. But my eyes... my eyes were different.\nThe fear was gone. The confusion was gone. In their place was a\nterrifying, absolute clarity. This is my path. This is my purpose. And\nno one, not even a god, is gonna stop me. I knew what we had to do. \"The\nDebug Vault,\" I whispered, the words tastin' of ozone and impossible\ntruths. \"We gotta get to the Debug Vault.\" Lennox, who had been standin'\nsilently in the doorway, a ghost drawn by the psychic flare, just\nnodded.\n\nHis thought entered all of our minds, a calm, steady, utterly insane\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754543188.7983503,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "052bfa54-77e8-45fe-a50c-abe595c67109",

    "08503417-e4f3-4241-92c8-e74933d3ac65": {
      "children": [
        "b872c669-13f2-4449-883b-1847c69c422a"
      ],
      "id": "08503417-e4f3-4241-92c8-e74933d3ac65",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "the source code. The communion lasted for a second or an eternity. When\nit was over, I was on the floor, gaspin', my mind reelin', a thin\ntrickle of blood from my nose. But my eyes... my eyes were different.\nThe fear was gone. The confusion was gone. In their place was a\nterrifying, absolute clarity. This is my path. This is my purpose. And\nno one, not even a god, is gonna stop me. I knew what we had to do. \"The\nDebug Vault,\" I whispered, the words tastin' of ozone and impossible\ntruths. \"We gotta get to the Debug Vault.\" Lennox, who had been standin'\nsilently in the doorway, a ghost drawn by the psychic flare, just\nnodded.\n\nHis thought entered all of our minds, a calm, steady, utterly insane\nconfirmation. The Architect has the map, he projected. But the child...\nthe child is the key.\n\nAct IV: Chapter 31 - The Inverse Manifesto \n\nThe silence in my mind ain't no peaceful lake. It's the vast, terrifying\nemptiness of a new country. The communion with Ember, it scoured me\nclean, not with fire, but with a cold, logical flood of pure data.\nRinsed away the last bits of the boy I was—the mechanic who saw the\nworld in gears and schematics—leavin' behind a vast, echoin' quiet,\nfilled with the ghosts of impossible data. I see the universe now, not\n"
          ]
        },

        "end_turn": null,

    "0ce92830-c5e5-40d2-8fe0-a0eaaee414fa": {
      "children": [
        "705a75ad-6286-4528-9a3b-966064f21cc0"
      ],
      "id": "0ce92830-c5e5-40d2-8fe0-a0eaaee414fa",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "\nThe lower markets of Te’Oga, they ain't just a place; they breathe. Like\na man after a long day, finally sittin' on his porch, watchin' the sun\ndrop and thinkin' about a cold beer. This whole damn market, it's alive.\nA beautiful, glorious, goddamn mess. Streets here don't give a damn\nabout blueprints or straight lines. They twist and turn, just like folks\nwho built 'em, worried more about a roof over their head than some fancy\narchitect's perfect angles. Every cobblestone's got a story soaked into\nit—deals cut, hearts busted, and enough cheap liquor drank to float a\nbattleship. Up high, you see those spires, all gleaming white, where the\nOrder lives. They're built on the idea that if you climb high enough,\nyou can leave all the world's bullshit behind. That's a lie, and every\ndamn soul down here knows it. The air in these markets? Smells like\ncoriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all\ncrammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell\nof life, the real kind, un-sanitized, un-approved. I move through this\ncrowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in\nmy pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a\nworld tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a\nmachine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can\nbe fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee-high to a\ngrasshopper, even after the Foundry. \"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\"\nThat's Samir, yellin' over the din. Voice like gravel and good whiskey.\nSpice merchant. Got a smile that could charm the chrome off a\nReclaimer's helmet, and a mechanical arm that clicks and clanks, a\ntestament to his own run-in with the world's sharp edges. He claps me on\nthe back with his good hand, the metal one cradlin' a crate of saffron\nlike it's a newborn. \"Thought the regulators finally got you. Three\ninspection teams swept through yesterday. Measured every stall. Fined\nold Mereen 'cause her counter was two centimeters too wide.\" Samir spits\non the ground, pure disgust. \"Two centimeters!\" I just nod, my eyes\ndriftin' up to those perfect spires. So clean. So white. So damn dead.\n\"What'd Mereen do?\" I ask, already knowin' the answer's gonna be good.\nSamir's grin is a thing of beauty, a gold tooth glintin' in the chaos.\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754547780.070831,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "0ce92830-c5e5-40d2-8fe0-a0eaaee414fa",

          "reasoning_status": "is_reasoning",

    "12aef2a1-152c-431f-9b8c-acf0bf945e68": {
      "children": [
        "074b2b02-30c5-42e1-acbd-cee9c0e470f7"
      ],
      "id": "12aef2a1-152c-431f-9b8c-acf0bf945e68",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "his voice hollow, the sound swallowed by the vast, new silence. \"Clean.\"\n\"Yes,\" I said, my own voice sounding distant, as if it belonged to\nsomeone else. \"Clean.\" It was only later, back at the temporary\nchapterhouse, cleaning the child's ash from the intricate joints of my\narmor, that I found the first crack in my faith. A hairline fracture,\nalmost invisible. But it was there. Roric had watched me, his young face\ntroubled, the certainty that had been so bright in his eyes now a faint,\nflickering thing. \"Sinner,\" he'd said, testing the word, the title they\nhad all given me with such pride. \"Is that what we are? The ones who\nkill children for the crime of being loved too much?\" I hadn't answered.\nI had just polished my blade, my movements precise, methodical, a\ndesperate attempt to rub away a stain that wasn't there. But the crack\nremained. And in the long, silent years that followed, it would grow, a\nbeautiful, terrible, and glorious flaw in the perfect, sterile armor of\nmy soul.\n\nAct I: Chapter 3 - The Flawed Gear \n\nThe lower markets of Te’Oga, they ain't just a place; they breathe. Like\na man after a long day, finally sittin' on his porch, watchin' the sun\ndrop and thinkin' about a cold beer. This whole damn market, it's alive.\nA beautiful, glorious, goddamn mess. Streets here don't give a damn\nabout blueprints or straight lines. They twist and turn, just like folks\nwho built 'em, worried more about a roof over their head than some fancy\narchitect's perfect angles. Every cobblestone's got a story soaked into\nit—deals cut, hearts busted, and enough cheap liquor drank to float a\nbattleship. Up high, you see those spires, all gleaming white, where the\nOrder lives. They're built on the idea that if you climb high enough,\nyou can leave all the world's bullshit behind. That's a lie, and every\ndamn soul down here knows it. The air in these markets? Smells like\ncoriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all\ncrammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell\nof life, the real kind, un-sanitized, un-approved. I move through this\ncrowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in\nmy pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a\nworld tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a\nmachine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can\nbe fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee-high to a\ngrasshopper, even after the Foundry. \"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\"\nThat's Samir, yellin' over the din. Voice like gravel and good whiskey.\nSpice merchant. Got a smile that could charm the chrome off a\nReclaimer's helmet, and a mechanical arm that clicks and clanks, a\ntestament to his own run-in with the world's sharp edges. He claps me on\nthe back with his good hand, the metal one cradlin' a crate of saffron\nlike it's a newborn. \"Thought the regulators finally got you. Three\ninspection teams swept through yesterday. Measured every stall. Fined\nold Mereen 'cause her counter was two centimeters too wide.\" Samir spits\non the ground, pure disgust. \"Two centimeters!\" I just nod, my eyes\ndriftin' up to those perfect spires. So clean. So white. So damn dead.\n\"What'd Mereen do?\" I ask, already knowin' the answer's gonna be good.\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754547805.2812588,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "12aef2a1-152c-431f-9b8c-acf0bf945e68",

    "1a1afdf6-4ba4-430a-b516-84cbb0663c97": {
      "children": [
        "1dfcbb25-5cd2-453e-bb1f-ff7552d0b6f4"
      ],
      "id": "1a1afdf6-4ba4-430a-b516-84cbb0663c97",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "war that had ended not in victory, but in a perfect, terrible, absolute\nstalemate. \"They're gone,\" Brighlee whispered, her voice a raw, broken\nsound. \"The people. The refugees. They're all gone.\" We'd \"saved\" 'em\nfrom the gods' cages, only to watch 'em be devoured by the bars. We\nlanded the Folly a few kilometers from the scar, the silence of the\nworld a heavy, suffocatin' blanket. The cost of our defiance was\nabsolute. We hadn't just lost a battle. We'd lost the battlefield\nitself. We sat around a cold, chemical light in the ship's cargo bay,\nthe ghosts of Meridian's last, defiant song echo in the quiet. The\nrefugees we'd pulled from Eries's forest, the ones who'd huddled by our\nfire just nights before, were gone, erased or absorbed with the rest of\nthe town. \"This was a mistake,\" Vael said, her voice a low, rough sound,\nthe words tastin' like ash. \"We shouldn't have interfered. We made it\nworse.\" \"No,\" I said, my voice quiet but firm. I was starin' at the\nbroken gear I'd forged, its missin' tooth a small, defiant shadow in the\ndim light. \"We gave 'em a choice. The gods... they were the ones who\ncouldn't handle the question.\" They were annihilated because they were\nthe battlefield, Lennox projected, his thought a cold, clear, merciless\npiece of logic. We did not bring the war to Meridian. We merely revealed\nthe bars of the cages that were already there. The flaw was not in our\ngambit. It was in our objective. We were trying to save the town. We\nshould have been trying to break the gods. The words hung in the air, a\nterrible and necessary truth. We'd been thinkin' like mortals, tryin' to\nwin a battle, to save a piece of ground. But we weren't fightin' a\nmortal war. We were fightin' a conceptual one. And in a war of ideas,\nyou don't win by takin' territory. You win by shatterin' the argument\nitself. \"So what now?\" Vael asked, her voice a low, dangerous growl. \"We\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754547854.7653599,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "1a1afdf6-4ba4-430a-b516-84cbb0663c97",

    "1c6634ea-a230-48b0-af1c-bbba4e21f1e0": {
      "children": [
        "1411d24a-5d0c-40ae-93b1-a7fc83dce171"
      ],
      "id": "1c6634ea-a230-48b0-af1c-bbba4e21f1e0",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "sputtered back to life. The silence they left behind was different. It\nwas heavier. Older. It was full of the beautiful, terrible, and glorious\ntruth of a universe choosing to rust.\n\nAct II: Chapter 15 \n\nThe great composer-Echo of the Panopticon was a peculiar figure. He was\nknown for his spare, minimalist compositions. His music was often\ndescribed as quiet, serene, and almost motionless. He wrote a piece\nonce, a haunting, beautiful work, that he titled Vexations. It was a\nsingle, short melody, a simple, four-note phrase, that he instructed\nshould be played 840 times in succession. The result, of course, was not\na song. It was an aural experience, a sonic meditation. A beautiful,\nterrible, and utterly maddening repetition that wore down the listener,\nexcising every thought, every feeling, every memory, until there was\nnothing left but the sound itself. It wasn't a song to be enjoyed; it\nwas a song to be endured. It was the sound of a beautiful, gilded prison\nfor the soul. And isn't that a beautiful, gilded lie? That the perfect,\nunwavering, and repetitive harmony is a form of peace, when it's really\njust a form of madness? After the Crown's collapse, after the scream\ndied, the world was left with a profound, ringing silence more deafening\nthan the roar. Vael pushed herself up, her body a collection of dull\naches, her mind a raw, scraped thing. She kicked the tavern door, now\nwarped and sagging in its frame, off its hinges and stepped outside into\na world wiped clean. The devastation was absolute, but strangely quiet.\nThe buildings still stood, silent and accusing under a sky that felt too\nbig. There were no bodies, no blood, no dust. The world hadn't been\ndestroyed; it had been erased. The chaos had been excised, the\nbeautiful, messy, and inefficient human story scrubbed from the page. It\nwas a world of perfect, sterile quiet. The sound of a beautiful, gilded\nlie. It was a world that had been \"fixed.\" And Vael, the Sinner of First\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754543176.3280208,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "1c6634ea-a230-48b0-af1c-bbba4e21f1e0",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {
            "changed_url": false,
            "generic_api_func": null,

            "subtool": null,

    "1e804f79-138f-4aff-a2d5-0ef584237e3f": {
      "children": [
        "18cd3286-8fef-4fba-981d-78661226839d"
      ],
      "id": "1e804f79-138f-4aff-a2d5-0ef584237e3f",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "messy, beautiful impurity of life. The Chorus would offer 'em a world\nwithout loneliness, without struggle, without the achin' burden of bein'\nyourself. The choice was between a perfect, sterile death and a\nbeautiful, empty peace. And a world in agony was startin' to find both\noptions lookin' pretty good. Aboard the Folly, we watched it all happen\non our scavenged sensor arrays. We were ghosts, hidin' in the edges of a\nworld tearin' itself apart, tryin' to find a path through the growin'\nmadness. \"They're not just fightin' each other,\" Brighlee muttered, her\nface lit by the flicker, chaotic map. \"They're eatin' the world. Ain't\ngonna be nothin' left to save.\" \"There was never anything to save,\" Vael\ngrunted from her perch, her voice a low, rough sound. \"There's only a\nchoice of cages.\" \"No,\" I said, my voice quiet but firm. I stood before\nthe map, the broken gear I'd forged held in my hand. \"There's another\nchoice.\" I looked at the faces of my small, fucked-up army. \"We ain't a\nfaction. We ain't a principle. We're the flaw. We're the question. And\nit's time we started givin' this world a new, much more interestin', set\nof answers.\" Answers these gods ain't ready for.\n\nChapter 35: The Deep Code \n\nI sit in the heart of the Folly's paradoxical engine, the big,\njury-rigged crystal capacitor pulsatin' with a soft, sorrowful light\naround me. I ain't just the ship's power source anymore; I'm its\nconsciousness, its soul. The communion with Ember, it unlocked somethin'\nin my mind, a door to a room I never knew existed, and now I'm tryin' to\nmake sense of the impossible, beautiful, terrifying furniture. I'm\nlearnin' to read the Deep Code. It flows through me, a constant, silent\nstream of information from Ember, filtered and translated by the\nstrange, harmonizin' resonance of Lennox's Order-tech amplifier. It\nain't like readin' a book or a schematic. I see the universe as a vast,\nintricate piece of music, a symphony of competin', paradoxical themes. I\nsee its laws of physics as lines of elegant, deeply flawed, code, a\nprogram that's constantly crashin' and rebootin' itself. The Operator's\ndesign is a closed system, I project to Lennox, who sits in quiet\nmeditation on the other side of the hummin' engine room. My mental voice\nis stronger now, clearer, the cobalt fire of my will givin' it a\nstrange, resonant sound. It was designed for a single purpose: to\nachieve a state of perfect, logical stasis. The 'gods' were the primary\nfunctions, but they were never meant to be autonomous. They were\nsubroutines, designed to keep the machine in balance. I show Lennox the\nvision from Ember's mind: the core programmin' of the universe, a\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754547860.3163905,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "1e804f79-138f-4aff-a2d5-0ef584237e3f",

          "n7jupd_subtool": {

            "generic_api_func": null,

            "subtool": null,
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "parent_id": "64b25676-be40-4216-b605-4a74d96ee6d4",
          "reasoning_group_id": "3852a8a4-6a3d-450a-81f4-b9def9c3bf02",
          "reasoning_status": "is_reasoning",
          "timestamp_": "absolute",

    "1e978e6d-686a-43ef-82b5-d842e6c8b52c": {
      "children": [
        "bbb2145c-be90-46c9-89fd-121b573b0b45"
      ],
      "id": "1e978e6d-686a-43ef-82b5-d842e6c8b52c",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "All requested enhancements—showcasing sound-dampening spires and posture-correcting announcements in Te’Oga, adding Samir’s defiantly imperfect stall, expanding the Scourlands with an amber fox, time river, and unsettlingly perfect grass, making the Bastard’s Folly respond to crew emotions and require a melody for its landing gear, weaving in Ember’s subtle humanity, providing a haunting confession from Lennox, and inserting quiet “campfire” scenes to deepen character textures—have been woven into the manuscript. The narrative flow and pacing remain intact while embedding the themes directly into the world-building. You can download the updated manuscript here: {{file:file-Cerbwd1LK8hFXt5pmBDsma}}.\n\nIf anything needs further refinement or adjustment, just let me know!"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754547985.012089,
        "end_turn": true,
        "id": "1e978e6d-686a-43ef-82b5-d842e6c8b52c",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": true,
          "citations": [],
          "content_references": [],
          "content_references_by_file": {},
          "is_complete": true,
          "n7jupd_crefs": [
            {
              "alt": null,
              "cite_key": "file-Cerbwd1LK8hFXt5pmBDsma",

            }
          ],
          "n7jupd_crefs_by_file": {},
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_schedulable": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {
            "changed_url": false,
            "generic_api_func": null,
            "result_of_subtool": null,
            "subtool": null,
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "n7jupd_summary": "The requested story revisions, including the integration of thematic elements such as sound-dampening spires and perfect societal structures in Te’Oga, Samir’s imperfect stall, and the ambient details in the Scourlands with mythical creatures and time-related phenomena, have all been completed. Additionally, I’ve revisited the interior of the Bastard’s Folly to reflect the crew's emotional influences and added subtle indications of Ember's humanity and Lennox’s internal struggles.\nThese improvements maintain narrative consistency while incorporating deeper character development and enriching the prose and pacing with carefully placed “campfire” scenes. The manuscript is now updated, and you can download it via the provided link. Let me know if further adjustments are needed!",
          "parent_id": "d047e9aa-472f-4b55-be79-ff2df81cdb52",
          "timestamp_": "absolute",

          "text": "{\"cmd\":[\"bash\",\"-lc\",\"python3 - <<'PY'\\nimport docx\\nfrom pathlib import Path\\npath = Path('/home/oai/share/Oddly Flaw\\\\'D.docx')\\ndoc = docx.Document(path)\\nprint('Paragraphs:', len(doc.paragraphs))\\nprint('First 20 paragraphs:')\\nfor i, p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs[:20]):\\n    print(i+1, p.text.strip())\\nPY\"], \"timeout\": 100000}"

    "27613aa1-5101-467e-b225-80700418a58f": {
      "children": [
        "cb6c8ab0-7270-4587-a943-74a7ed73cf39"
      ],
      "id": "27613aa1-5101-467e-b225-80700418a58f",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "file_search",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "tether_quote",
          "domain": "Oddly Flaw'D.docx",
          "tether_id": null,
          "text": "fileciteturn0file2\n\nPrologue: The Cosmic Argument \n\nLet's get one thing straight right outta the gate. The beginning wasn't the beginning. Not really. The kind they write in books, all clean calendars and tidy morals, that's just a story we tell ourselves to sleep better at night. This story, the real one, the one that's written in the marrow of the whole damn thing, it started before the first clock had the guts to tick. It started when the universe was little more than a pressure in the dark. A heavy thought in a mind that wasn't a mind yet. Before time was a concept, before there was a \"before,\" there was this… this will. A pure, stubborn, unyieldin' son of a bitch called the Counter-Spark. Now, this fella's ambition, his only truth, was to press the entire goddamn universe into a single, flawless, and perfectly dead crystal. A vast, silent emptiness just longin' for more emptiness. A real drag at a party, you know the type. All logic, no soul. A perfect equation that added up to nothin'. It was the peace of a perfect, sterile tomb, and it ached for that peace with a longing that was the only feeling in a \n\nfeelingless void. Then, a gear slipped. Somethin' inside the great, quiet machine just... broke. A raw, gut-level, \"I AM\" tore through that silence like a bottle rocket in a fucking library, and it let the light in. That first defiant scream, that first beautiful \"fuck you\" to the void, that was the First Spark. And it called itself Xilcore, the God of Perfect Form. And man, he shone. I mean, he shone so bright you'd get a sunburn on your fucking soul just lookin' at him. With a voice like two mountains grindin' together, he spoke the first star into being. Just like that. A monument to his own flawless logic. A perfect, beautiful, goddamn diamond in the dark. But here's the kicker, the part they leave out of the sermons. In his very first act of creation, in that one perfect moment... he made a mistake. A shadow bled from the edge of that perfect star. And this wasn't some sleepy afternoon shadow; this thing had teeth. It drank the warmth right out of the newborn air. See, in givin' the universe Form, in makin' it something, Xilcore had accidentally given Formlessness a name. He'd drawn a line in the sand, and in doin' so, he'd created the other side. That first, fundamental flaw... well, that was the starting gun for the whole damn race. From that friction, that sweet, necessary grind between bein' and nothin', others woke up. Leesa, the Goddess of Wholeness, she shimmered up from the star’s own perfect symmetry, a being of pure, placid light. She saw the argument between the light and the shadow and felt it as a physical pain, a tear in the fabric of what should be a seamless whole. She just wanted to smooth it all out, to get everyone to hug it out and be one big, happy, frictionless family. Her peace was a beautiful, seductive lie, the promise of an end to all struggle, all pain, all self. A smiling tyrant in a world that was just learnin' how to bleed. Then there was Blemo, the God of Purity. He crystallized from the star's unbendin' laws, a being of pure, antiseptic logic. He saw the shadow not as a necessary contrast, but as a contamination. A flaw. A disease. His whole deal was purgin' those flaws, like a surgeon cuttin' out a tumor, thinkin' you could make the world clean by gettin' rid of all the dirt. He was a doctor so focused on the disease, he couldn't see the patient was the whole damn world. And from the simple, honest promise that all things, eventually, have to break, Seeri, the Goddess of Decay, she crawled into being. She was the necessary rot, the force of change that kept the universe from becoming a static, boring museum of its own perfection. But her power, you understand, it was a cancer. Left unchecked, it'd forget the difference between prunin' a dead branch and just burnin' the whole goddamn forest down. These four... Xilcore, Leesa, Blemo, Seeri... they were born from that first cosmic argument. Their war is one of existence itself, a slow, grindin' friction that kicks off a chaotic energy, a static that mortals, in all their wisdom, call the Divine Sickness. And mortals, bein' who they are, they responded by formin' the Reclaimers, an order sworn to purge this sickness. They believed they could fix a world that was designed, from the get-go, to be flawed. A noble effort. A real fool’s errand. And now, one of their best, a woman with more ghosts in her eyes than a confederate graveyard, is walkin' into a damp, stinkin' cave, thinkin' she's the cure… when she’s really just another beautiful, tragic, and utterly necessary symptom. \n\nChapter 1: Vael: The Ash in the Soul \n\nThe first god I ever tasted was a low, dissonant hum that vibrated up through the soles of my worn-out boots. It was a flavor like a rusty nail under your tongue for too long, a sharp, metallic tang more a memory than a physical sensation. This god, this goddamn infection on the world, was a permanent stain on the air, and my weapon, the Boneblade, pulsed with a quiet, hungry energy against my back. The thing only ever wanted one thing: more. It was a perfect, terrible, and utterly honest companion. I wasn’t a god-killer back then. I was a child. I was eight years old. I remember the sound of my little brother's laughter, a bright, happy thing, until it twisted and soured into a discordant shriek, a note on a broken string, before his small body just… dissolved. Turned to a cloud of shimmering green dust that caught the afternoon light in a way that was so beautiful, so tragically perfect, it was horrifying. The stench of that Divine Sickness, a miasma that smelled of overripe fruit and burnt hair, is the first thing I remember. It was my baptism, a raw, gut-level, \"I AM\" that ripped through the silence of my childhood. A moment, I've come to realize, that was both the end of my life and the true beginning of it. A week later, the Order came. They were a line of black ink drawn across the crumpled page of my ruined village, their polished armor an accusation. Father Morr, their leader, a man whose face looked like it was carved from granite and regret, spoke of purity and cleansing. He was a promise of order in a world of screaming chaos. I didn't want prayers. I wanted a cure. I wanted a goddamn weapon. I joined the Order a year later, a furious twelve-year-old with eyes that had seen too much to ever be young again. I was a beautiful monster in the making, and I was thrilled to get started. My training was a special kind of hell, designed to burn away the girl and forge a tool. Kora, my mentor, was a grim-faced woman whose hands were calloused from prayer and violence in equal measure. She taught me the \"flavors\" of the Sickness, the different ways the divine could curdle a soul. The smooth river rock that hummed with a placid quiet? \"That's Leesa's poison,\" she'd grunt. \"A Siren's song promising a frictionless peace. It'll absorb you, smooth you out, until there's nothin' left of you but a quiet echo.\" The jagged obsidian that radiated a sterile, calculating silence? \"And that's Blemo's logic-blight. The Surgeon's promise of perfection. It'll carve away every flaw, every feeling, every memory, until you're a perfect, clean, and utterly empty machine.\" Kora looked me dead in the eye, her own gaze a flat, hard thing. \"Your job isn’t to understand them, little sinner. Your job is to recognize them. And to hate them. That hate will keep you sharp. It's the only thing that will.\" I never forgot that. The hate was a beautiful, terrible gift. My first purge was a minor \"grief-storm.\" A farmer’s sorrow had festered, turning his fields to a black, bubbling ichor. We found him wreathed in a visible aura of despair. \"He is a symptom,\" Kora said, her voice devoid of pity. \"The sickness is the disease. The man is just the fever.\" I didn't hesitate. With a grim, cold satisfaction, I buried the Boneblade in his chest. The blade drank down the chaotic energy, and the man, the fields, the very air itself, seemed to sigh in relief, leaving only sterile dust and a profound, unsettling quiet behind. It was an exquisite, efficient, and utterly clean act of violence. I knew then that I had found my purpose. Later, Kora put a hand on my shoulder. \"You have a gift for this, child. A capacity for the necessary sin.\" She called me a \"great Sinner.\" I embraced the title, of course. I was a magnificent tool, a glorious instrument of the world's inevitable, and necessary, ruin. For years, I was the \"Sinner of First Flame,\" an efficient, remorseless purger who never questioned her purpose. I was a hammer, and the world was just full of nails I had to pound flat. Until Brinefall. The job was a footnote in a long list of purges. A damp, rotting village on the coast, suffering under some local superstition they called a \"Kraken-God.\" I took their coin, a collection of salt-crusted silver and worn-down copper, and prepared for another day at the office. On my way out of the village, I passed two young Reclaimers, their armor still shiny, their faces still full of a belief I'd long since traded for efficiency. They muttered my title with a wary respect, seeing the legend but not the ash that was starting to collect in the lines around my eyes. The landscape itself felt sick. The trees clawed at the earth, their branches twisted into agonized shapes, as if some bored god had leaned in too close and left a stain. I found the lair in a stinking sea cave, the entrance choked with the smell of blood, cheap ceremonial wine, and rotting fish guts. Offerings were piled at the entrance, and among them, a crudely carved wooden soldier, its paint worn away by a child's hands. My jaw clenched. I hated the gods that demanded toys; I found their sentimental cruelty particularly offensive. The divine resonance inside was frantic, a sputtering, desperate energy like a dying engine trying to turn over one last time. This wasn't the crushing, self-assured weight of a true god. This was just noise. A parasite, feeding on scraps. I recognized the \"flavor\" of its demand: a warped, pathetic echo of Leesa’s placid peace, a desperate craving for absorption. At the heart of the cave, I found not a kraken, but a man. Gaunt and half-mad, wrapped in fishing nets like a holy shroud, he stood over a crude stone altar where a young boy lay unnervingly still. The man held a shard of obsidian, a sliver of night, and was siphoning the child’s life force with a pathetic, borrowed spark of divinity. I didn't bother with an introduction. \"Turn around,\" I said, my voice cutting through his ragged, desperate chanting like a shard of glass. He shrieked, a thin, reedy sound, and brandished his obsidian knife. \"You cannot stop the Tidemother's baptism! He will be made whole!\" I saw the whole sad story in his wild, terrified eyes. A fisherman whose grief for a lost family at sea had curdled into this monstrous, pathetic shape. \"There is no Tidemother,\" I said, my voice flat and hard. \"There's just you.\" The fight was a joke. A pathetic, clumsy tragedy. He swung the knife in a wild arc, and I easily dodged, cracking him on the wrist with the butt of my staff. His final mistake was charging me bare-handed, a scream of pure, impotent rage on his lips. I pivoted, a simple, economical movement, and thrust the Boneblade into his chest. It found the stolen spark of divinity and drank it down with a thirsty hum. The man collapsed, the madness in his eyes replaced by a horrifying, final clarity as he died. It was a beautiful, terrible moment, a final, perfect act of sorrow. I pulled my blade free, feeling the hollow echo where his power had been. It left a new kind of emptiness inside me, a cold ash in the wound of my own spirit. I turned to the altar. The boy was alive, a thin trickle of blood running from a shallow cut on his arm. I knelt, my knees cracking in the sudden silence. I tore a strip from my tunic and gently, almost tenderly, bandaged the boy's thin arm. He looked from the dead man on the floor to me, his eyes wide, but he didn't scream. I said nothing. I wasn't a savior. I was the Sinner of First Flame, a broken tool who only knew how to take things apart. As I lifted the boy into my arms and carried him out of that stinking, sad excuse for a church, the Boneblade was silent against my back, its hunger sated for now. My hunger, I was beginning to realize, was a whole different kind of animal. And it was just getting started. \n\nChapter 2: Vael: The Zealot's Oath \n\nThe air in the Sanctum of the First Flame tasted like burnt sugar and melted gold. It was a holy place, which meant it was a place of profound, divine violence. I, younger then, my Reclaimer armor still polished to a mirror shine, my faith a hard, clean blade in my gut, breathed it in like incense. This was the work. This was justice. This was the beautiful, terrible, and necessary business of making the world clean. It was a sublime, theatrical cruelty, and I was its star. Father Morr's command had been simple, his voice a calm, grinding certainty that could level mountains and build new ones from the rubble. \"The sickness in Aeridor is a nascent god,\" he'd said, his gaze a heavy, physical weight on my shoulders. \"A child-thing, glutted on the city's faith. It has become a cancer in the Pattern, a beautiful, sentimental, and utterly deadly flaw. You will be the cure, Vael. You will be my scalpel. Excise the tumor.\" My squad moved through the molten corridors of the sanctum, a place that had once been the city's primary treasury. The child-god, in its innocent, terrible power, had taken the cold, hard logic of wealth and turned it into a living, breathing work of art. The gold and coin of a kingdom had been melted down into a new, living architecture. Rivers of liquid gold flowed in intricate, beautiful patterns on the walls, the heat a physical, oppressive blow that made the air shimmer and dance. The very stones of the place seemed to hum with a joyful, chaotic energy. My men were on edge, their armor's cooling runes working overtime, their breath hissing through their helmet vents. \"Feels wrong,\" one of them, a young Reclaimer named Roric whose face was still too soft for the hard lines of his armor, muttered over the comm. \"Feels like we're walking through a dream.\" \"Dreams can be sick, too,\" I had snapped, my voice sharp, a clean, hard line in the humming chaos. \"Stay focused.\" But I felt it, too. The place wasn't malevolent. It wasn't angry. It was... playful. The divine resonance was pure, joyful, and utterly without malice. It was the sound of a child's laughter, amplified to the power of a dying star. And it was the most dangerous thing I had ever encountered. We found him in the central vault, a great, domed chamber that had once held the crown jewels of a forgotten dynasty. He was sitting on a throne of half-melted brass and copper, a small, chubby boy no older than six, radiating a power so immense it made the air itself feel thick and heavy. He was playing with a ball of pure, molten light, giggling as it changed shape in his small hands, shifting from a bird to a flower to a star. This was the Blasphemy of Aeridor. A child who had accidentally, beautifully, and terribly, become a god. \"Hold the perimeter,\" I commanded, my voice a flat, cold crack in the humming, joyful silence. The men fanned out, their resonance lances primed, their movements precise, economical, a testament to the Order's beautiful, terrible logic. I walked forward alone. The Boneblade was new to me then, a fresh, hungry thing, and it pulsed with a low, eager energy, tasting the raw, untamed divinity in the air. This was a feast. The child-god looked up, his eyes the color of hot coals. He didn't look afraid. He looked curious. He held out the ball of light, an offering. A toy. Sinner. Butcher. Breaker. The words weren't there then. They would come later, echoes in the ash of my soul, ghosts that would haunt me in the quiet hours of the night. In that moment, there was only the mission. The cold, clean logic of the Reclaimers. The Sickness must be purged. A flaw in the Pattern must be corrected. Tolerance is a weakness. I felt the memory of Morr's hand on my shoulder, the weight of his absolute, unwavering certainty. I was his finest tool. His Sinner of First Flame. The one he sent when the sickness was too subtle, too sympathetic, for a blunt instrument. \"I am sorry,\" I said, and I meant it. It was the last time I would ever feel pity for a target. Pity was a flaw. Pity was a weakness. Pity was a sickness of the soul. A luxury I had yet to fully earn. The child tilted his head, not understanding the words, but understanding the intent in my heart, the cold, clean purpose in my will. The ball of light in his hand flickered and went out. His lower lip began to tremble. For the first time, he looked afraid. He was just a child. A beautiful, tragic, and utterly delicious mistake. I raised the Boneblade. The weapon knew what to do. It sang a high, keen note of anticipation, a beautiful, terrible, and hungry sound. I didn't hesitate. I was a surgeon, and this was a tumor. The strike was fast, clean, merciful. The Boneblade found the divine spark in the child's chest and drank it down in one, greedy gulp. The searing light of the sanctum vanished. The rivers of gold on the walls froze and cracked, the beautiful, living architecture dying in an instant, becoming just dull, worthless metal. The heat died, replaced by a profound, and terrible, cold. The child-god didn't scream. He made a small, surprised sound, a soft sigh, and then he was gone, dissolving into a fine, gray ash that settled on the floor in a small, perfect, and silent pile. It was a breathtaking tableau of absolute and terrible stillness. I had done my job beautifully. I stood over the empty throne, the Boneblade thrumming with a deep, satisfying pulse, its hunger sated. I had done it. I had cleansed the sickness. I had been the perfect tool. The squad moved in, their faces grim, their boots crunching on the newly cooled, and now worthless, gold. Roric stared at the pile of ash, his expression unreadable behind his helmet's visor. \"It's done,\" he said, his voice hollow, the sound swallowed by the vast, new silence. \"Clean.\" \"Yes,\" I said, my own voice sounding distant, as if it belonged to someone else. \"Clean.\" It was only later, back at the temporary chapterhouse, cleaning the child's ash from the intricate joints of my armor, that I found the first crack in my faith. A hairline fracture, almost invisible. But it was there. Roric had watched me, his young face troubled, the certainty that had been so bright in his eyes now a faint, flickering thing. \"Sinner,\" he'd said, testing the word, the title they had all given me with such pride. \"Is that what we are? The ones who kill children for the crime of being loved too much?\" I hadn't answered. I had just polished my blade, my movements precise, methodical, a desperate attempt to rub away a stain that wasn't there. But the crack remained. And in the long, silent years that followed, it would grow, a beautiful, terrible, and glorious flaw in the perfect, sterile armor of my soul. \n\nAct I: Chapter 3 - The Flawed Gear \n\nThe lower markets of Te’Oga, they ain't just a place; they breathe. Like a man after a long day, finally sittin' on his porch, watchin' the sun drop and thinkin' about a cold beer. This whole damn market, it's alive. A beautiful, glorious, goddamn mess. Streets here don't give a damn about blueprints or straight lines. They twist and turn, just like folks who built 'em, worried more about a roof over their head than some fancy architect's perfect angles. Every cobblestone's got a story soaked into it—deals cut, hearts busted, and enough cheap liquor drank to float a battleship. Up high, you see those spires, all gleaming white, where the Order lives. They're built on the idea that if you climb high enough, you can leave all the world's bullshit behind. That's a lie, and every damn soul down here knows it. The air in these markets? Smells like coriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all crammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell of life, the real kind, un-sanitized, un-approved. I move through this crowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in my pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a world tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a machine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can be fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee-high to a grasshopper, even after the Foundry. \"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\" That's Samir, yellin' over the din. Voice like gravel and good whiskey. Spice merchant. Got a smile that could charm the chrome off a Reclaimer's helmet, and a mechanical arm that clicks and clanks, a testament to his own run-in with the world's sharp edges. He claps me on the back with his good hand, the metal one cradlin' a crate of saffron like it's a newborn. \"Thought the regulators finally got you. Three inspection teams swept through yesterday. Measured every stall. Fined old Mereen 'cause her counter was two centimeters too wide.\" Samir spits on the ground, pure disgust. \"Two centimeters!\" I just nod, my eyes driftin' up to those perfect spires. So clean. So white. So damn dead. \"What'd Mereen do?\" I ask, already knowin' the answer's gonna be good. Samir's grin is a thing of beauty, a gold tooth glintin' in the chaos. \"What we always do. She paid the fine\nThe file is too long and its contents have been truncated.",
          "title": "Oddly Flaw'D.docx",
          "url": "file-4nCLxjuhdL4Ly5i5fKPbtX"
        },

    "2ce9b973-c9aa-4e5b-8756-070eada8fe2e": {
      "children": [
        "e996c7c8-caf6-446c-9dac-0b2059c9440c"
      ],
      "id": "2ce9b973-c9aa-4e5b-8756-070eada8fe2e",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "  2430\tand repetitive harmony is a form of peace, when it's really just a form\n  2431\tof madness?\n  2432\t\n  2433\tAfter the Crown's collapse, after the scream died, the world was left\n  2434\twith a profound, ringing silence more deafening than the roar. Vael\n  2435\tpushed herself up, her body a collection of dull aches, her mind a raw,\n  2436\tscraped thing. She kicked the tavern door, now warped and sagging in its\n  2437\tframe, off its hinges and stepped outside into a world wiped clean. The\n  2438\tdevastation was absolute, but strangely quiet. The buildings still\n  2439\tstood, silent and accusing under a sky that felt too big. There were no\n  2440\tbodies, no blood, no dust. The world hadn't been destroyed; it had been\n  2441\terased. The chaos had been excised, the beautiful, messy, and\n  2442\tinefficient human story scrubbed from the page. It was a world of\n  2443\tperfect, sterile quiet. The sound of a beautiful, gilded lie. It was a\n  2444\tworld that had been \"fixed.\"\n  2445\t\n  2446\tAnd Vael, the Sinner of First Flame, the one who had spent her life\n  2447\ttrying to burn away the flaws, was left standing alone in the center of\n  2448\ta world that was now a flawless, and perfectly dead, thing. She had a\n  2449\tmoment of beautiful, terrible clarity then. She wasn't a doctor, she was\n  2450\ta grave digger. And she had just buried the entire world.\n  2451\t\n  2452\tThe truth is, I remember the silence more than the scream. The silence\n  2453\twas worse. The silence was the sound of a promise that had been broken,\n  2454\ta truth that had been lost. The silence was the sound of my own choices,\n  2455\techoing in an empty world. And it was deafening.\n  2456\t\n  2457\tI've spent years trying to understand what happened that day. Trying to\n  2458\trationalize it, to analyze it, to break it down into a sequence of\n  2459\tlogical events that I could understand and categorize. But the truth is,\n  2460\tsome things cannot be understood. They can only be experienced. They can\n  2461\tonly be felt.\n  2462\t\n  2463\tThe Crown didn't just fall. It wasn't just destroyed. It was unmade.\n  2464\tErased from the fabric of reality itself. And in its place, a new\n  2465\tpattern emerged. A new song. A new, terrible, and beautiful reality.\n  2466\t\n  2467\tI think about Alya sometimes. About the way she used to laugh when I\n  2468\twould try to explain my theories, my elegant models of reality. \"The\n  2469\tuniverse isn't a problem to be solved,\" she would say. \"It's a song to\n  2470\tbe sung.\"\n  2471\t\n  2472\tI thought she was being poetic. Inefficient. Illogical. But now,\n  2473\tstanding in the silence of a world that has forgotten how to sing, I\n  2474\twonder if she was right all along.\n  2475\t\n  2476\tMaybe the universe doesn't want to be fixed. Maybe it wants to be broken\n  2477\tin a more interesting way.\n  2478\t\n  2479\tMaybe the flaw isn't a bug in the system. Maybe it's the entire point.\n  2480\t\n  2481\tI don't know if I can bear that truth. I don't know if I can accept that\n  2482\tall my work, all my plans, all my perfect, elegant models, were not just\n  2483\twrong, but fundamentally, existentially misguided. That I wasn't just\n  2484\tsolving the wrong problem; I was creating it.\n  2485\t\n  2486\tBut I do know one thing: the silence cannot hold. The song will return.\n  2487\tAnd when it does, it will not be a song of perfect, sterile harmony. It\n  2488\twill be a wild, chaotic, and gloriously imperfect symphony of a universe\n  2489\tremembering how to dream.\n  2490\t\n  2491\tAnd I don't know if I will be a conductor or a listener. A participant\n  2492\tor a witness. A creator or a destroyer.\n  2493\t\n  2494\tBut I do know this: I will be there. And I will not turn away. Not this\n  2495\ttime.\n  2496\t\n  2497\tThe pencil is heavy in my hand, but I keep writing. The words are messy,\n  2498\timperfect, full of contradictions and illogic. But they are alive. And\n  2499\tin a world that has forgotten how to sing, perhaps that is enough.\n  2500\t\n  2501\t# Act II: Chapter 16\n  2502\t\n  2503\tListen up, because this is how you know you're in business. When your\n  2504\tplan, your beautiful, goddamn flawless plan, goes to shit. It ain't\n  2505\tabout the plan. It's about the pivot. It's about lookin' at a dumpster\n  2506\tfire and seein' a bonfire.\n  2507\t\n  2508\tThe Bastard's Folly, our beautiful piece of shit spaceship, didn't so\n  2509\tmuch land in the Glass Peaks as it got caught. An invisible hand, some\n  2510\tkind of divine puppeteer, just yanked us right out of the sky. The\n"
          ]
        },

    "2e345993-aa01-4b29-bc6e-9d768295bb49": {
      "children": [
        "e10013a0-6858-4973-9c37-7e0720643b8f"
      ],
      "id": "2e345993-aa01-4b29-bc6e-9d768295bb49",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Prologue: The Cosmic Argument\n\nLet’s get one thing straight right outta the gate. The beginning wasn’t\nthe beginning. Not really. The kind they write in books, all clean\ncalendars and tidy morals, that’s just a story we tell ourselves to\nsleep better at night. This story, the real one, the one that’s written\nin the marrow of the whole damn thing, it started before the first clock\nhad the guts to tick. It started when the universe was little more than\na pressure in the dark. A heavy thought in a mind that wasn’t a mind\nyet.\n\nBefore time was a concept, before there was a “before,” there was this…\nthis will. A pure, stubborn, unyieldin’ son of a bitch called the\nCounter-Spark. Now, this fella’s ambition, his only truth, was to press\nthe entire goddamn universe into a single, flawless, and perfectly dead\ncrystal. A vast, silent emptiness just longin’ for more emptiness. A\nreal drag at a party, you know the type. All logic, no soul. A perfect\nequation that added up to nothin’. It was the peace of a perfect,\nsterile tomb, and it ached for that peace with a longing that was the\nonly feeling in a feelingless void.\n\nThen, a gear slipped. Somethin’ inside the great, quiet machine just…\nbroke. A raw, gut-level, “I AM” tore through that silence like a bottle\nrocket in a fucking library, and it let the light in. That first defiant\nscream, that first beautiful “fuck you” to the void, that was the First\nSpark. And it called itself Xilcore, the God of Perfect Form.\n\nAnd sweet divine glory, he shone. I mean, he shone so bright you’d get a\nsunburn on your fucking soul just lookin’ at him. With a voice like two\nmountains grindin’ together, he spoke the first star into being. Just\nlike that. A monument to his own flawless logic. A perfect, beautiful,\ngoddamn diamond in the dark.\n\nBut here’s the kicker, the part they leave out of the sermons. In his\nvery first act of creation, in that one perfect moment… he made a\nmistake. A shadow bled from the edge of that perfect star. And this\nwasn’t some sleepy afternoon shadow; this thing had teeth. It drank the\nwarmth right out of the newborn air.\n\nSee, in givin’ the universe Form, in makin’ it something, Xilcore had\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754543071.0820234,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "2e345993-aa01-4b29-bc6e-9d768295bb49",

    "34480275-5501-421e-ab4d-4f9036f27393": {
      "children": [
        "f2257272-f0f7-4bc3-a1fe-be3d9ccc1b4e"
      ],
      "id": "34480275-5501-421e-ab4d-4f9036f27393",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "  2090\tglassblower, she knew that in order to make something perfect, you have\n  2091\tto start with a flaw. She just needed to find the right one. She just\n  2092\tneeded the right piece of broken glass.\n  2093\t\n  2094\tThe truth is, I was the Architect. And the flaw I was trying to fix...\n  2095\tit was me. It was all the messy, emotional, and illogical parts of me\n  2096\tthat I was trying to burn away. I thought if I could just get rid of the\n  2097\trust in the gears, the machine would run perfectly. I thought if I could\n  2098\tjust find the right lie to tell myself, I could be a hero.\n  2099\t\n  2100\tBut the flaw wasn't just in me. It was in the entire concept of\n  2101\tperfection itself. The universe doesn't want to be fixed. It doesn't\n  2102\twant to be perfect. It wants to be alive.\n  2103\t\n  2104\tI'm trying to write about Tiffani, but all I can see is Alya. The ghost\n  2105\tin my perfect machine. The beautiful chaos I tried to erase.\n  2106\t\n  2107\tHer last words to me, before I calibrated her out of existence, were not\n  2108\ta plea or a curse. They were a promise.\n  2109\t\n  2110\t\"You can't fix this,\" she said, her voice already fading as my scalpel\n  2111\tof logic cut away at her pattern. \"You can only break it into something\n  2112\tnew.\"\n  2113\t\n  2114\tI thought she was wrong. I thought I could find the perfect algorithm,\n  2115\tthe flawless equation that would solve for every variable. I thought I\n  2116\tcould write a world without pain, without loss, without the messy,\n  2117\tbeautiful chaos of being alive.\n  2118\t\n  2119\tI was a monster with a blueprint, and I called it salvation.\n  2120\t\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754543225.8646863,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "34480275-5501-421e-ab4d-4f9036f27393",

    "36b132f4-7a49-4d22-8158-d45b19f68ebf": {
      "children": [
        "638c28bb-12b7-43a2-b202-3208b04a913d"
      ],
      "id": "36b132f4-7a49-4d22-8158-d45b19f68ebf",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "  2080\tof reality itself, her interface projecting a looping spiral of\n  2081\tcollapsing simulations—dozens of futures, all ending in ash, each one a\n  2082\tdifferent flavor of failure. She didn't see the world as a place to live\n  2083\tin; she saw it as a problem to be solved, a flawed, inefficient, and\n  2084\tbeautifully broken program that just needed the right code to run\n  2085\tperfectly. Her ambition was not just to fix the world, but to create a\n  2086\tnew one from the ashes of the old. She believed, with a cold, clean fire\n  2087\tin her gut, that the only way to save the world was to kill it first.\n  2088\t\n  2089\tHer logic was an impeccable, beautiful thing. And like the master\n  2090\tglassblower, she knew that in order to make something perfect, you have\n  2091\tto start with a flaw. She just needed to find the right one. She just\n  2092\tneeded the right piece of broken glass.\n  2093\t\n  2094\tThe truth is, I was the Architect. And the flaw I was trying to fix...\n  2095\tit was me. It was all the messy, emotional, and illogical parts of me\n  2096\tthat I was trying to burn away. I thought if I could just get rid of the\n  2097\trust in the gears, the machine would run perfectly. I thought if I could\n  2098\tjust find the right lie to tell myself, I could be a hero.\n  2099\t\n  2100\tBut the flaw wasn't just in me. It was in the entire concept of\n  2101\tperfection itself. The universe doesn't want to be fixed. It doesn't\n  2102\twant to be perfect. It wants to be alive.\n  2103\t\n  2104\tI'm trying to write about Tiffani, but all I can see is Alya. The ghost\n  2105\tin my perfect machine. The beautiful chaos I tried to erase.\n  2106\t\n  2107\tHer last words to me, before I calibrated her out of existence, were not\n  2108\ta plea or a curse. They were a promise.\n  2109\t\n  2110\t\"You can't fix this,\" she said, her voice already fading as my scalpel\n  2111\tof logic cut away at her pattern. \"You can only break it into something\n  2112\tnew.\"\n  2113\t\n  2114\tI thought she was wrong. I thought I could find the perfect algorithm,\n  2115\tthe flawless equation that would solve for every variable. I thought I\n  2116\tcould write a world without pain, without loss, without the messy,\n  2117\tbeautiful chaos of being alive.\n  2118\t\n  2119\tI was a monster with a blueprint, and I called it salvation.\n  2120\t\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754543103.423354,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "36b132f4-7a49-4d22-8158-d45b19f68ebf",

    "3a3cbf88-3daf-4e81-9d89-465f8474420c": {
      "children": [
        "072f92ca-51af-42d6-9f2b-643de93ac59c"
      ],
      "id": "3a3cbf88-3daf-4e81-9d89-465f8474420c",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "  3270\tBreak it! Break it all!\" He looked at the two faltering armies, at the\n  3271\ttwo gods whose absolute truths had just been challenged. He held up the\n  3272\tbroken gear he had forged, and he smiled, a raw, broken, and beautiful\n  3273\tthing. He gave the memory of Meridian teeth. He wove Brighlee's rage and\n  3274\tmy defiance into the song, creating a terrible hymn of glorious\n  3275\timperfection. The no-man's-land erupted. The air became a weapon. It\n  3276\twasn't a sermon. It was a riot. A revolution aimed at two gods armed\n  3277\twith the one thing they couldn't compute: a choice. And it was a\n  3278\tmagnificent, and tragic, piece of theater.\n  3279\t\n  3280\tAct IV: Chapter 38 - The Cost of a Question \n  3281\t\n  3282\tYou know, a lot of people think a good argument is one where somebody\n  3283\twins. Where one side stands tall and the other one gets buried. But\n  3284\tsometimes... sometimes a truly good argument just tears the whole\n  3285\tgoddamn table apart. It's not about who's right. It's about what happens\n  3286\twhen both sides get so caught up in their own certainty, that they\n  3287\tforget what they're supposed to be fightin' for. We didn't win that day.\n  3288\tBut we gave 'em a question they'd never thought to ask. And for a little\n  3289\twhile, that was enough. The riot of memory and defiance held for twelve\n  3290\tbeautiful, terrible seconds. It was a glorious, chaotic \"fuck you\" aimed\n  3291\tat the heavens, a brief, shinin' moment where three broken mortals and a\n  3292\tghost held two gods at bay. Then the gods answered. And their answer was\n  3293\tabsolute. Mobel, his patience for this messy, inefficient impurity worn\n  3294\tout, raised his hand. His will, a scalpel the size of a mountain, came\n  3295\tdown on\n  3296\t\n  3297\tMeridian. He didn't try to purge the chaos I'd unleashed. He purged the\n  3298\tcanvas it was painted on. The northern half of the town, the part the\n  3299\tBrotherhood had \"cleansed,\" just ceased to be. The white-washed\n  3300\tbuildings, the sterile streets, the very ground they stood on, dissolved\n  3301\tinto a fine, sterile, utterly featureless grey dust. It wasn't an\n  3302\texplosion. It was an erasure. A final, perfect, silent solution to a\n  3303\tmessy problem. Salee, her placid harmony shattered by Vael's defiant\n  3304\tblade, responded with a sorrowful, all-consumin' love. The southern half\n  3305\tof the town, her golden collective, didn't dissolve. It integrated. The\n"
          ]
        },

    "3c2190c4-4b8c-4114-857d-15517b26c1e6": {
      "children": [
        "bb5265f1-c56c-4201-a89a-1a8678ad2cf4"
      ],
      "id": "3c2190c4-4b8c-4114-857d-15517b26c1e6",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "damn soul down here knows it. The air in these markets? Smells like\ncoriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all\ncrammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell\nof life, the real kind, un-sanitized, un-approved. I move through this\ncrowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in\nmy pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a\nworld tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a\nmachine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can\nbe fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee-high to a\ngrasshopper, even after the Foundry. \"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\"\nThat's Samir, yellin' over the din. Voice like gravel and good whiskey.\nSpice merchant. Got a smile that could charm the chrome off a\nReclaimer's helmet, and a mechanical arm that clicks and clanks, a\ntestament to his own run-in with the world's sharp edges. He claps me on\nthe back with his good hand, the metal one cradlin' a crate of saffron\nlike it's a newborn. \"Thought the regulators finally got you. Three\ninspection teams swept through yesterday. Measured every stall. Fined\nold Mereen 'cause her counter was two centimeters too wide.\" Samir spits\non the ground, pure disgust. \"Two centimeters!\" I just nod, my eyes\ndriftin' up to those perfect spires. So clean. So white. So damn dead.\n\"What'd Mereen do?\" I ask, already knowin' the answer's gonna be good.\nSamir's grin is a thing of beauty, a gold tooth glintin' in the chaos.\n\"What we always do. She paid the fine, bless her stubborn heart. Smiled\nthat sweet, toothless smile of hers. And the second those shiny-booted\nsons of bitches were gone, she got her saw, cut the damn counter... and\nmade it three centimeters wider on the other side.\" We both laugh, a\nreal, honest laugh. That's rebellion, right there. The market's full of\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1754547811.4608724,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "3c2190c4-4b8c-4114-857d-15517b26c1e6",

          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "from docx import Document\r\n\r\n# Paths to the cleaned rewritten parts\r\nparts = [\r\n    \"/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_clean_part1.docx\",\r\n    \"/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_clean_part2.docx\",\r\n    \"/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_clean_part3.docx\",\r\n    \"/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_clean_part4.docx\",\r\n    \"/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_clean_part5.docx\"\r\n]\r\n\r\n# Create a new document for the compilation\r\ncompiled = Document()\r\n\r\nfor part in parts:\r\n    doc = Document(part)\r\n    # For each paragraph in the part, add it to the compiled document\r\n    for para in doc.paragraphs:\r\n        compiled.add_paragraph(para.text)\r\n    # Add a page break between parts\r\n    compiled.add_page_break()\r\n\r\n# Save the compiled document\r\noutput_path = \"/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_compiled.docx\"\r\ncompiled.save(output_path)\r\n\r\n# Provide the download link to the user\r\noutput_path"
        },

    "3e28c477-6983-45a0-8e1d-53f0632ae9f0": {

        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Order lives. They're built on the idea that if you climb high enough,\nyou can leave all the world's bullshit behind. That's a lie, and every\ndamn soul down here knows it. The air in these markets? Smells like\ncoriander, engine oil, grilled meat, and ozone. A thousand lives all\ncrammed together, makin' one impossible, workin' smell. That's the smell\nof life, the real kind, un-sanitized, un-approved. I move through this\ncrowd like a fish in water, my fingers rubbin' that worn, flawed gear in\nmy pocket. My touchstone, my rosary. A little piece of imperfection in a\nworld tryin' its damnedest to be perfect. The universe, I figure, is a\nmachine, and any machine, no matter how busted, can be figured out. Can\nbe fixed. That's a truth I've held onto since I was knee-high to a\ngrasshopper, even after the Foundry. \"Jhace! You magnificent bastard!\"\nThat's Samir, yellin' over the din. Voice like gravel and good whiskey.\nSpice merchant. Got a smile that could charm the chrome off a\nReclaimer's helmet, and a mechanical arm that clicks and clanks, a\ntestament to his own run-in with the world's sharp edges. He claps me on\nthe back with his good hand, the metal one cradlin' a crate of saffron\nlike it's a newborn. \"Thought the regulators finally got you. Three\ninspection teams swept through yesterday. Measured every stall. Fined\n"
          ]
        },

        "end_turn": null,

          "n7jupd_subtool": {

    "40a47109-5a88-47b5-94c6-55c72c1264e7": {
      "children": [
        "057caac6-c600-48e5-b68a-32b724fd94b9"
      ],
      "id": "40a47109-5a88-47b5-94c6-55c72c1264e7",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "{\"cmd\":[\"bash\",\"-lc\",\"python - <<'PY'\\nfrom docx import Document\\nimport re, math\\nimport os\\n\\n# Load docx\\ninput_docx_path = '/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_updated.docx'\\n\\ndoc = Document(input_docx_path)\\nparas = [p.text for p in doc.paragraphs]\\n\\n# transformation\\nreplacements = {\\n        \\\"ain’t\\\": \\\"aren’t\\\",\\n        \\\"ain't\\\": \\\"aren't\\\",\\n        \\\"gonna\\\": \\\"going to\\\",\\n        \\\"I'm\\\": \\\"I am\\\",\\n        \\\"I'll\\\": \\\"I will\\\",\\n        \\\"you're\\\": \\\"you are\\\",\\n        \\\"can't\\\": \\\"cannot\\\",\\n        \\\"don't\\\": \\\"do not\\\",\\n        \\\"I've\\\": \\\"I have\\\",\\n        \\\"isn't\\\": \\\"is not\\\",\\n}\\n\\nheading_prefixes = ('Prologue', 'Chapter', 'Act', 'Interlude', 'Epilogue')\\n\\ndef transform_paragraph(paragraph: str) -> str:\\n    if not paragraph.strip():\\n        return paragraph\\n    if paragraph.lstrip().startswith(heading_prefixes):\\n        return paragraph\\n    parts = re.split('([.!?])', paragraph)\\n    new_sentences = []\\n    for i in range(0, len(parts),2):\\n        text = parts[i].strip()\\n        delim = parts[i+1] if i+1 < len(parts) else ''\\n        if not text:\\n            continue\\n        for k,v in replacements.items():\\n            text = text.replace(k, v)\\n        text = ' '.join(text.split())\\n        sentence = text + delim + \\\" Trust me.\\\"\\n        new_sentences.append(sentence)\\n    result = ' '.join(new_sentences)\\n    return \\\"Here's the thing: \\\" + result\\n\\ntransformed_paras = [transform_paragraph(p) for p in paras]\\n\\n# split into 5 parts\\nnum_paras = len(transformed_paras)\\nparts = 5\\nsize = (num_paras + parts - 1)//parts\\npaths = []\\nfor i in range(parts):\\n    start = i*size\\n    end = min((i+1)*size, num_paras)\\n    out_doc = Document()\\n    for p in transformed_paras[start:end]:\\n        # Add a paragraph; docx library automatically creates paragraphs\\n        if p == '':\\n            out_doc.add_paragraph()\\n        else:\\n            out_doc.add_paragraph(p)\\n    file_path = f'/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_part{i+1}.docx'\\n    out_doc.save(file_path)\\n    paths.append(file_path)\\n\\nprint('Created:', paths)\\nPY\"],\"timeout\": 100000}"
        },
        "create_time": 1754554673.20356,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "40a47109-5a88-47b5-94c6-55c72c1264e7",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "citations": [],
          "content_references": [],
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {
            "changed_url": false,
            "generic_api_func": null,
            "result_of_subtool": null,
            "subtool": "python",
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "reasoning_group_id": "84ec34c2-b146-4677-8dc6-d75bedcfde1a",
          "reasoning_status": "is_reasoning",
          "timestamp_": "absolute",
          "turn_id": "a10a0d53-6870-480f-8cd9-90652b3a4c0f"

    "40c6a90c-b5b1-4429-a681-e20777edbbdc": {
      "children": [
        "57fca39b-a0bc-4a6c-8b23-d7acada8f1c9"
      ],
      "id": "40c6a90c-b5b1-4429-a681-e20777edbbdc",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "unknown",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "{\"cmd\":[\"bash\",\"-lc\",\"python - <<'PY'\\nfrom docx import Document\\nimport re, math\\n\\n# read original docx\\ninput_docx_path = '/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_updated.docx'\\ndoc = Document(input_docx_path)\\nparas = [p.text for p in doc.paragraphs]\\n\\n# replacements dictionary for professional grammar\\nreplacements = {\\n    \\\"ain’t\\\": \\\"is not\\\",\\n    \\\"ain't\\\": \\\"is not\\\",\\n    \\\"gonna\\\": \\\"going to\\\",\\n    \\\"I'm\\\": \\\"I am\\\",\\n    \\\"I've\\\": \\\"I have\\\",\\n    \\\"I'll\\\": \\\"I will\\\",\\n    \\\"we've\\\": \\\"we have\\\",\\n    \\\"you're\\\": \\\"you are\\\",\\n    \\\"they're\\\": \\\"they are\\\",\\n    \\\"can't\\\": \\\"cannot\\\",\\n    \\\"don't\\\": \\\"do not\\\",\\n    \\\"won't\\\": \\\"will not\\\",\\n    \\\"didn't\\\": \\\"did not\\\",\\n    \\\"it's\\\": \\\"it is\\\",\\n    \\\"that's\\\": \\\"that is\\\",\\n    \\\"there's\\\": \\\"there is\\\",\\n    \\\"we're\\\": \\\"we are\\\",\\n    \\\"doesn't\\\": \\\"does not\\\",\\n    \\\"isn't\\\": \\\"is not\\\",\\n    \\\"wasn't\\\": \\\"was not\\\",\\n}\\n\\n# headings prefix\\nheading_prefixes = ('Prologue', 'Chapter', 'Act', 'Interlude', 'Epilogue')\\nfirst_body_done = False\\ntrust_me_used = False\\n\\ntransformed = []\\n\\nfor p in paras:\\n    # preserve blank lines\\n    if not p.strip():\\n        transformed.append(p)\\n        continue\\n    if p.lstrip().startswith(heading_prefixes):\\n        transformed.append(p)\\n        continue\\n    # apply replacements for grammar\\n    text = p\\n    for k, v in replacements.items():\\n        # use regex for whole words case-insensitive\\n        text = re.sub(r'\\\\b' + re.escape(k) + r'\\\\b', v, text)\\n    # remove repeated spaces\\n    text = ' '.join(text.split())\\n    # Add preface to first body paragraph only\\n    if not first_body_done:\\n        text = 'Let me level with you: ' + text\\n        first_body_done = True\\n    # Insert 'trust me' phrase once in entire manuscript near early part\\n    if not trust_me_used and 'Let me level with you' in text:\\n        # Append trust me once at end of the paragraph\\n        text += ' Trust me.'\\n        trust_me_used = True\\n    transformed.append(text)\\n\\n# split into 5 parts\\nnum = len(transformed)\\nparts = 5\\nsize = (num + parts - 1)//parts\\nfiles = []\\nfor i in range(parts):\\n    start = i * size\\n    end = min((i+1)*size, num)\\n    part = transformed[start:end]\\n    doc_out = Document()\\n    for t in part:\\n        doc_out.add_paragraph(t)\\n    file_path = f'/home/oai/share/final_hybrid_rewritten_clean_part{i+1}.docx'\\n    doc_out.save(file_path)\\n    files.append(file_path)\\nprint(files)\\nPY\"],\"timeout\": 100000}"
        },
        "create_time": 1754554797.948557,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "40c6a90c-b5b1-4429-a681-e20777edbbdc",
